---
title: "Your Paper Title"
author:
  - name: Author One
    orcid: 0000-0000-0000-0001
    email: author.one@example.org
    affiliations:
      - name: Department, University, City, Country
  - name: Author Two
    orcid: 0000-0000-0000-0002
    email: author.two@example.org
    affiliations:
      - name: Department, University, City, Country
  - name: Author Three
    orcid: 0000-0000-0000-0003
    email: author.three@example.org
    corresponding: true
    affiliations:
      - name: Department, University, City, Country
date: today
date-format: "[This revision:] D MMMM YYYY"
abstract: |
  This is a self-documenting Quarto paper template. The body of the
  paper explains how to use the qkit paper format: YAML metadata,
  cross-references, equations, theorem environments, citations,
  callouts, code chunks, tables, figures, and the in-paper
  Appendix. Replace this abstract with your own (150--250 words
  covering question, data, method, and main findings).

  \bigskip
  \noindent\textbf{Keywords:} keyword one, keyword two, keyword three

  \noindent\textbf{JEL Classification:} A00, B00, C00
keywords:
  - keyword one
  - keyword two
  - keyword three
thanks: |
  We thank seminar and conference participants for helpful comments.
  Replace this acknowledgments block with your own.
bibliography: references.bib
filters:
  - mark-last-author.lua

format:
  pdf:
    pdf-engine: xelatex
    documentclass: article
    classoption:
      - "12pt"
    geometry:
      - margin=1in
    linestretch: 2
    indent: true
    fontsize: 12pt
    colorlinks: true
    linkcolor: red
    citecolor: blue
    urlcolor: magenta
    number-sections: true
    keep-tex: true
    subparagraph: true
    template-partials:
      - title.tex
    include-in-header: preamble.tex
---

```{=tex}
\newpage
```

# Introduction {#sec-intro}

This template is a worked example of Quarto's PDF authoring
features as configured by the **qkit Paper** scaffold. Each section
below demonstrates a different feature; replace the demonstration
text with your own prose when you fill the template in.

## Editing the YAML header

The block of metadata above the body (between the two `---`
markers) is the YAML header. The qkit Paper template wires it up
so the author block is fully editable from YAML --- no LaTeX
required for routine changes:

``` yaml
author:
  - name: Author One
    orcid: 0000-0000-0000-0001
    email: author.one@example.org
    affiliations:
      - name: Department, University, City, Country
  - name: Author Two
    ...
  - name: Author Three
    corresponding: true
    ...
```

Each author becomes a name followed by a small `\orcidlink`
superscript and a `\thanks{}` footnote with the email (wrapped in
a clickable `mailto:` link) and the address. Setting
`corresponding: true` on one author adds *"Corresponding author."*
to that author's footnote. The italic *"Preliminary draft ---
please do not cite without permission"* subtitle is appended to
the title by the same `title.tex` partial.

To add or remove an author, just add or remove a YAML list entry.
The first ORCID renders as `^1`, the second `^2`, and so on (the
LaTeX `\thanks` mechanism numbers them automatically).

## Markdown basics

Use single asterisks for *italic*, doubles for **bold**, triples
for ***bold italic***, and backticks for `inline code`. Em dashes
are typed as three hyphens `---`, en dashes as two `--`, and an
ellipsis as three dots `...`. Hyperlinks are inline: see the
[Quarto documentation](https://quarto.org).

Smart-quote handling is on by default, so `"text"` becomes
"text" with proper curly quotes and `'text'` becomes 'text'.

## Cross-references

Prefix any heading label with one of Quarto's cross-reference
prefixes:

| Prefix | Used for | Reference syntax |
|--------|----------|------------------|
| `sec-` | Sections | `@sec-intro` |
| `eq-`  | Equations | `@eq-baseline` |
| `tbl-` | Tables | `@tbl-summary` |
| `fig-` | Figures | `@fig-example` |

The remainder of the paper is organized as follows. Section
@sec-theory shows how to write theorems and labeled equations.
Section @sec-data shows how to insert tables. Section @sec-method
demonstrates citations and equation references. Section
@sec-results covers Quarto callouts. Sections @sec-additional and
@sec-robust cover quotations, footnotes, and code formatting.
Section @sec-conc closes. Appendices @sec-app-vardesc and
@sec-app-derivations demonstrate `\appendix`-style numbering.

## Citations

Cite a `.bib` entry with `[@key]` for a parenthetical (Doe and Roe
2025) or with `@key` for an in-text citation; the qkit paper
template uses Pandoc's `citeproc` (Chicago author-date by
default). The bibliography renders at the `# References` anchor
placed after the Conclusion. For instance, @example2025 introduces
the ideas summarized here.

# Theoretical Framework {#sec-theory}

A labeled display equation uses the syntax
`$$...$$ {#eq-label}`. For example:

$$
y_{it} = \alpha_i + \delta_t + \beta\, x_{it} + \gamma'\mathbf{Z}_{it} + \varepsilon_{it}
$$ {#eq-baseline}

is referenced inline with `@eq-baseline`. The qkit preamble forces
symmetric vertical space above and below every display equation,
so the gap looks the same whether the line preceding the equation
is short or long.

For multi-line displays, wrap in an `aligned` environment inside
the `$$ $$`:

$$
\begin{aligned}
y_{it} &= \alpha_i + \delta_t + \beta\, x_{it} + \varepsilon_{it} \\
\varepsilon_{it} &\sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^{2}).
\end{aligned}
$$ {#eq-aligned}

`cases`, `pmatrix`, `bmatrix`, and the rest of the `amsmath`
toolkit are available because the document loads `amsmath` by
default.

Five theorem-style environments are predefined via `amsthm`:
`theorem`, `proposition`, `lemma`, `definition`, and `assumption`.
Use any with Pandoc's fenced-div syntax `::: {.theorem}` ... `:::`.

::: {.assumption}
The error term $\varepsilon_{it}$ is mean-zero and conditionally
independent of the focal regressor $x_{it}$ given the unit fixed
effect $\alpha_i$.
:::

::: {.proposition}
Under the assumption above, the within-unit OLS estimator of
$\beta$ in @eq-baseline is consistent.
:::

## Testable Predictions {#sec-theory-predictions}

Subsection headings render italic, not bold --- a deliberate qkit
style choice that gives section headings the visual weight and
subsection headings the contrast of italic. Section numbering
carries a period after the digit (`1. Introduction`, `1.1
Subsection`), also a preamble tweak.

# Data {#sec-data}

Variable definitions are deferred to Appendix @sec-app-vardesc.
This section shows how to insert a table directly in markdown and
via a `knitr` chunk.

## Data Sources and Sample Construction {#sec-data-sources}

Markdown bullet lists work as expected:

- One bullet per line, hyphen + space at the start.
- Sub-bullets indent four spaces.
    - Like this nested entry.
- Empty lines between items create a "loose" list with extra space
  between entries.

## Variable Definitions {#sec-data-vars}

Numbered lists use the same syntax:

1. Use `1.`, `2.`, `3.` at the start of each item.
2. Numbering is automatic; Quarto renumbers on render.
3. To restart numbering, leave a blank line and start a new list.

Definition lists are written with the term on its own line and the
definition indented two spaces below, prefixed by a colon:

Term
:   Definition of the term goes here. Multiple lines stay attached
    to the same term as long as the indentation is consistent.

Another term
:   Its definition.

## Summary Statistics {#sec-data-summary}

Reference Table @tbl-pipe for descriptive statistics. The first
example below is a **pipe table** written in pure markdown:

| Variable | Mean | SD |
|----------|-----:|---:|
| Outcome $y$ | 0.50 | 0.10 |
| Focal regressor $x$ | 0.00 | 1.00 |
| Control $z^{1}$ | 0.00 | 1.00 |

: Pipe-table example. {#tbl-pipe}

The alignment symbols `:-`, `-:`, `:-:` after each header dash
control left, right, and centre alignment of the column.

The second example uses a **`knitr` chunk** for a computed table.
With `eval: false` it stays a placeholder; flip to `true` and add
real data to render a `knitr::kable()` table:

```{r}
#| label: tbl-summary
#| tbl-cap: "Summary statistics for the example panel."
#| echo: false
#| eval: false
# knitr::kable(your_data_frame, booktabs = TRUE)
```

Chunk options live in the `#| key: value` block at the top of the
chunk. `label:` drives cross-references (use `tbl-` for tables,
`fig-` for figures), `tbl-cap:` sets the caption, and `eval:`
controls whether the code actually runs.

# Methodology {#sec-method}

To cite a reference, write `@key` for in-text or `[@key]` for
parenthetical. Multiple citations stack inside a single
parenthetical: `[@key1; @key2; @key3]`. To suppress the author
name in a citation, prefix with a hyphen: `[-@key]` --- useful when
the author has already been mentioned in the sentence.

The estimator solves a nonlinear least-squares problem:

$$
\widehat{\beta} = \arg\min_{\beta} \sum_{i,t} \bigl(y_{it} - X_{it}'\beta\bigr)^{2}.
$$ {#eq-nls}

Standard errors are clustered at the unit level, following
@example2025.

# Main Results {#sec-results}

This section demonstrates Quarto callouts. Five variants are
available; each renders as a tinted box with a coloured left rule.

::: {.callout-note}
A `note` callout is for general asides --- context the reader
might want but doesn't need to follow the main argument.
:::

::: {.callout-tip}
A `tip` callout flags a practical recommendation or shortcut.
:::

::: {.callout-warning}
A `warning` callout highlights a caveat or limitation.
:::

::: {.callout-important}
An `important` callout draws attention to something the reader
must not miss.
:::

::: {.callout-caution}
A `caution` callout is the strongest emphasis variant; use it
sparingly.
:::

## Baseline Estimates {#sec-results-baseline}

Estimating @eq-baseline on the full sample yields a point estimate
of $\widehat{\beta} = 0.12$ (standard error 0.03). Quarto knows
that `@eq-baseline` should render as a parenthetical equation
reference because the label was declared with the `eq-` prefix.

## Heterogeneity {#sec-results-hetero}

Subgroup estimates range from 0.04 to 0.21. Inline math uses
single dollar signs: $\widehat{\beta}_{\text{low}} = 0.04$ and
$\widehat{\beta}_{\text{high}} = 0.21$.

## Mechanisms {#sec-results-mechanism}

Two-thirds of the headline effect operates contemporaneously; the
remainder unfolds with a one-period lag.

# Additional Analyses {#sec-additional}

Block quotations are rendered with 1.5cm indentation on both sides
--- a qkit preamble tweak that matches the look of standard
working-paper templates:

> Quoted material goes here. The custom `quote` environment is
> indented an extra 1.5cm on each side relative to the body text,
> matching a common econ/finance convention. Multiple lines stay
> within the block as long as each starts with `>`.

Footnotes use the inline syntax `^[...]`.^[Like this --- a
footnote attached to the word "this". Footnotes render at the
bottom of the page they appear on.]

# Robustness {#sec-robust}

Code spans use single backticks (`` `code` ``); fenced code blocks
use triple backticks with an optional language tag for syntax
highlighting:

```latex
\textit{italic text} and \textbf{bold text}.
```

```r
fit <- lm(y ~ x + z1 + z2 + z3, data = df)
summary(fit)
```

Inline math: $\widehat{\beta} \xrightarrow{p} \beta$ as $n \to
\infty$. Display math: see @eq-baseline.

For LaTeX-only material that should not be processed as markdown,
use a raw block:

```` markdown
```{=tex}
\begin{flushright}\itshape Author's note.\end{flushright}
```
````

The same syntax with `{=html}` produces HTML-only material when
you render to HTML. To make content appear only in one output
format, use a fenced div with `.content-visible`:

```markdown
::: {.content-visible when-format="pdf"}
This block appears only in the PDF render.
:::
```

# Conclusion {#sec-conc}

A paper template that documents itself: each section above
demonstrates a different Quarto feature. When you replace the
demonstration prose with your own analysis, the headings,
equations, theorem environments, citations, callouts, and quotes
will pick up the qkit styling automatically.

```{=tex}
\newpage
```

# References {.unnumbered}

::: {#refs}
:::

```{=tex}
\newpage
\appendix
\setcounter{table}{0}
\setcounter{figure}{0}
\renewcommand{\thesection}{Appendix \Alph{section}}
\renewcommand{\thetable}{\Alph{section}.\arabic{table}}
\renewcommand{\thefigure}{\Alph{section}.\arabic{figure}}
\renewcommand{\theequation}{\Alph{section}.\arabic{equation}}
```

# Variable Definitions {#sec-app-vardesc}

The in-paper Appendix demonstrates appendix-specific numbering.
The `\appendix` macro --- called from a raw-LaTeX block just
before this heading --- resets the section counter and remaps
`\thesection` so headings render as "Appendix A.", "Appendix B.",
and so on. Tables, figures, and equations within an appendix
section pick up the appendix letter as a prefix (A.1, A.2, B.1,
...).

In a real paper, this section would carry a `tabularx` or
`booktabs` table listing every variable used in @sec-data, with
its definition, units, and source.

# Auxiliary Derivations {#sec-app-derivations}

A display equation inside Appendix B renders as Equation B.1:

$$
\widehat{\beta} = (X'X)^{-1} X' y.
$$ {#eq-app-baseline}

Compare to @eq-baseline in the main text --- that one is numbered
plainly (e.g. 1, 2, ...) while @eq-app-baseline carries the
B-prefix.

Proofs use a `.proof` div, which `amsthm` renders with an italic
"Proof." header and a closing $\square$:

::: {.proof}
*Sketch.* Within-unit demeaning eliminates the fixed effect; the
demeaned regressor satisfies the orthogonality condition stated in
the assumption; OLS on the demeaned variables is therefore
consistent. $\square$
:::
